entry.title: SQL: Left position Vs. Right position in JOINS
entry.content:
Hello,
In the below SQL in the 6th line the 'b' (CSKT) has been specified in the left postion but whereas in all the rest (5,7 and 8 of the lines the 'b' has been specified in right position, so in this case pls. let us know wheter this SQL is ok? is it pulls correct data? is it pulls ALL the qualfied records? Thanks

SELECT a~kokrs a~bukrs a~kostl b~ltext
  INTO TABLE (lt_cost_data)
  FROM csks AS a
  LEFT JOIN cskt AS b
  ON  a~kokrs EQ b~kokrs
  AND b~spras EQ sy-langu "Here I took 'B'(CSKT) in left position, is it ok?  
  AND a~kostl EQ b~kostl        
  AND a~datbi EQ b~datbi         
  WHERE a~kokrs EQ   p_kokrs    
  AND a~kostl   IN   s_kostl 
  AND a~bukrs   IN   s_bukrs  
  AND a~datbi   GE  sy-datum   
  AND a~datab   LE  sy-datum.

entry.title: The Autonomous Enterprise and Intelligent Feedback Ecosystem—Powered by Your Feedback
entry.content:
Do you recognize the footpaths branching off the walkway in this photo?

Urban planners call these “desire paths”, and see them as valuable data, not defiance. Many start with desire paths, then build walkways where people have shown they are needed.

Desire paths echo the first of three important trends in our industry. It’s how we—SAP and our customers—are shaping future SAP products together. Here is a look into these three important trends:

1. SAP Customers and Users are becoming co‑creators through feedback, intent and intelligence

Just as human needs shape paths through a landscape—AI-supported products increasingly allow customers to shape their workflows through product feedback, expressed intent and configuration more than traditional feature requests.

Users convey desired workflow and outcomes and systems adapt (“I need this process in a different order, so that I can start here and end up with this output and that outcome.”). This transforms requestors into co-creators. Product boundaries ultimately become fluid, dynamically composed by AI agents.

2. Continuous in‑context feedback

While customer councils and other high-touch engagements are tremendously valuable, continuous in‑context feedback collection—like Joule chat and our PX In-Product Surveys—add low-touch sentiment and urgency signals, giving us depth where needed and scale where possible. Our transformation is built on this foundation of over 12 million in-product feedback records collected since 2020, including 4.4 million in 2025 alone.

Adding multimodal signals like usage telemetry and user behavior analytics helps build a holistic view of users’ desire paths.

3. AI‑augmented insight turns noise into decision‑grade signals

As feedback volumes and data sets continue to grow, AI-powered analytics tools help us see patterns that matter, where to investigate further, and where to act. Our AI platform helps us cluster and triangulate feedback at scale, with telemetry and user behavior analytics validating feedback topics against observed user behavior.

Market-driven, customer-centric roadmaps are impacted by our Continuous Influence services. Innovation flows from real user and customer feedback, not boardroom assumptions—breaking new ground and removing obstacles along the way.

These inputs, other crucial market signals, and the data relationships they reveal through analytics, help us prioritize actions and investments based on strategic goals, context and maximizing customer value.


Our vision: An Intelligent Feedback Ecosystem

We are harmonizing the most important feedback and telemetry data channels to reduce noise and maximize insights as we transform the development lifecycle into an innovation flywheel that moves the Autonomous Enterprise.

AI-powered analytics are central to this transformation—finding insights in sentiment, intent and behavior for actions that maximize the value of customer investments.

Product teams balance feedback, telemetry, other market signals, strategy, and product standards. Not all feedback is actionable immediately, but closing the loop with customers is vital to an intelligent feedback ecosystem.

Knowing where your feedback went, and the impact it made, grows trust and commitment to keep sharing feedback.

So please keep sharing your feedback and engage in SAP's Customer Influence services. Encourage users to leave in-product feedback. These are all vital steps on the path to future enterprise experiences.


Join us at SAP Sapphire in Madrid!

We would love to hear your comments and questions below—and to discuss this shared future in person
during our Strategy Talk —  Feedback to Delivery: How Customer Feedback Shapes SAP Products
and at our Demo Station at SAP Sapphire Madrid!

entry.title: Announcing SAP Inside Track Bengaluru 30th May 2026
entry.content:
We are excited to invite you to SAP Inside Track (SIT) Bengaluru on May 30th, 2026! This year, we are offering an exclusive opportunity to explore the future of SAP Business AI—the forefront of innovation.

Join us as we delve into the world of Business AI: learn how to build AI solutions on SAP BTP, discover how partners and customers are leveraging SAP Business AI, and explore various SAP solutions with embedded AI through hands-on, insightful sessions and demos.

Event Overview

SAP Inside Track 2026 – Business AI Edition aims to provide CXOs, Leaders, Developers, Consultants, Architects, and IT Managers with a deep understanding of how to effectively integrate Business AI with SAP products.

This event is your gateway to unlocking the full potential of SAP and AI within your organization.

What is SAP Inside Track?

SAP Inside Track is a community-driven event where SAP professionals, developers, consultants, and leaders gather to share knowledge and insights on SAP-related topics. Organized by members of the SAP Community, it provides a platform for fostering connections, staying updated on SAP technologies, and gaining recognition for your technical expertise.
Know more about SAP Inside Track Here!

Event Details:  

Date: May 30th, 2026 
Time: 8 AM - 5 PM IST 
Venue: SAP Labs India, 138, SAP Labs Rd, EPIP Zone, Whitefield, Bengaluru, Karnataka 560066 

Registration:  

Don't miss out on this incredible opportunity to be part of Bengaluru's largest SAP community event!

Register Now!
Note: This is an in-person event only.

Agenda:  

This SAP Inside Track will be a full-day exploration of SAP Business AI. You will:

  • Learn to build AI-powered applications on SAP Business Technology Platform.
  • Discover innovative Business AI solutions from leading partners.
  • Witness the transformative power of Embedded AI through live demos across SAP solutions.

Our event features expert-led sessions by SAP, partners, and customers. Plus, a dedicated hands-on workshop zone and demo booths showcasing AI use cases.

Stay tuned for the full agenda, which will be posted soon! Join the SAP Community Bengaluru group to stay updated!

Speaker Registration:

Speaker registrations are now open! If you're an expert in building AI applications on SAP BTP, developing innovative Business AI solutions, or working with embedded AI in SAP solutions like SAP S/4HANA or SuccessFactors, we welcome your contribution. We also invite partners and customers to showcase their work.
Register as a speaker here!

Exclusive Sponsorship Opportunities:

With over 4,000 community members across locations in the past 12 months, we’re eager to continue growing. If you're interested in sponsoring the event, please comment below, and we’ll reach out to you. This year, we have exclusive sponsor bundles that offer opportunities to showcase your content and expertise across multiple locations, including Bangalore, Gurgaon, Hyderabad, and Pune.

Join the Bengaluru SAP Community Group:

If you're in Bengaluru or Karnataka, join the Bengaluru SAP Community Group to engage in discussions, share ideas, and collaborate with fellow SAP enthusiasts.
Stay connected with us on Twitter and LinkedIn for updates on this and future community events.

Best Regards,
Ranjeet

entry.title: New Course Announcement: Getting Started with SAP Enterprise Consent and Preference Management
entry.content:
We’re excited to introduce a new course in the SAP Customer Data Cloud learning portfolio:

Getting Started with SAP Enterprise Consent and Preference Management

This course is the next step in your journey after completing
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management 

Together, these courses form part of a comprehensive learning experience designed to guide you through the full implementation of SAP Customer Data Cloud.

 Part of a Complete Learning Journey

This course is part of a broader enablement plan for SAP Customer Data Cloud.

⚠️Note: This course will soon be officially included in this Learning Journey.

Once fully released, the learning journey will contain four new complementary courses, covering the complete scope of the instructor-led training (ILT):

SAP Customer Data Cloud Implementation

These courses collectively guide you through identity, access, consent, and advanced configuration topics—providing a complete end-to-end learning experience.

Who Should Take This Course?

This course is designed for:

  • Customer data professionals (intermediate level) 
  • Consultants and practitioners working with customer data platforms
  • Professionals responsible for privacy, compliance, and consent management

If you’re already familiar with identity and access management concepts, this course will help you extend your expertise into privacy and preference management.

What You’ll Learn

In today’s privacy-first world, managing consent is critical. This course provides a practical, hands-on introduction to SAP Enterprise Consent and Preference Management capabilities.

By the end of the course, you’ll be able to:

  • Understand global privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA) and their impact 
  • Configure and manage customer consent using SAP Customer Consent
  • Apply version control to ensure valid and up-to-date consent records 
  • Use the Consent Vault to track, audit, and report consent activity 
  • Set up and customize a Self-Service Preference Center for end users [
  • Define and manage communication channels and topics to personalize user preferences 

Ultimately, you’ll gain the skills to implement enterprise-grade consent strategies that support compliance while enabling trusted customer engagement. [

 Course Highlights

The course is structured into three practical units:

1. Foundations of Customer Consent Management

  • Key regulations and compliance requirements
  • Core principles of consent and user rights

2. Managing Consent with SAP Customer Data Cloud

  • SAP Customer Consent capabilities
  • Version control and consent lifecycle
  • Consent Vault for auditing and reporting
  • Self-Service Preference Center configuration

3. Communication Preferences and Personalization

  • Communication channels and topics
  • Subscription management and user choice

🔗Prerequisite Course

Before taking this course, we strongly recommend completing:

 Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management
This foundational course introduces identity concepts and prepares you to fully understand consent and preference management within SAP Customer Data Cloud.

Why This Course Matters

As organizations operate across global markets, compliance with privacy regulations is no longer optional. Customers expect transparency, control, and trust.

With SAP Enterprise Consent and Preference Management, you can:

  • Ensure regulatory compliance
  • Build trust-based digital relationships
  • Deliver personalized yet privacy-aware experiences

This course equips you with the knowledge and practical skills to make that happen.

Start Learning Today

Enroll now:
Getting Started with SAP Enterprise Consent and Preference Management

Stay tuned—more CIAM courses are coming soon.

entry.title: Re: Generate 1 cheque from multiple payment document with...
entry.content:
Dear @PAAA,

Thank you for the clarification. Yes, the requirement of one cheque for INV001, INV002 and INV003 of Company ABC is supported in Public Cloud but it must be solved at the payment document level. The relationship between a payment document and a cheque is strictly 1:1 by design, so one cheque covering three separately posted payment documents (PV001, PV002, PV003) is not possible. The three invoices must instead be paid by a single payment document during the Automatic Payment Program run, which will then receive one cheque automatically

To achieve this the payment proposal in Manage Automatic Payments must select and group INV001, INV002 and INV003 together. The grouping requires that no splitter settings are active on the supplier or on the cheque payment method the relevant checks are in the BP Supplier Company Code segment, SSCUI 101044 and SSCUI 101001 as described in the previous response and the cheque form settings (Payment Advice Note Control) remain aligned

If PV001, PV002 and PV003 have already been posted separately the correction is to void the issued cheques in Manage Checks (F1578 Manage Outgoing Checks), reverse the three payment documents to reopen the invoices and rerun the Automatic Payment Program. The system will then produce one consolidated payment document and one cheque for Company ABC

More details below:

https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('F1578')/S36

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAP Datasphere – Replication Flow – Loading delta data from SAP S/4HANA – Q & A Session
entry.content:
Hi Fernando,

please find:  

SAP Datasphere Replication Flows - PS/QM CDS Views Gap Analysis

The Core Problem

You're absolutely right. For modules like PS (Project System) and QM (Quality Management), SAP hasn't delivered dedicated CDS extraction views with the @analytics.dataExtract or @DataWarehouse.delta annotations needed for Datasphere Replication Flows.

PS Extractors vs CDS View Mapping

Project Master Data Extractors

BW Extractor Description CDS View Option Status

0PS_PROJECT_ATTRProject Definition AttributesI_ProjectDefinition⚠️ Partial
0PS_WBS_ELMT_ATTRWBS Element AttributesI_WorkPackage / I_WBSElementBasicData⚠️ Partial
0PS_MILESTON_ATTRMilestone AttributesI_ProjectMilestone⚠️ Partial
0PS_NETWORK_ATTRNetwork HeaderI_MaintenanceOrder (workaround)❌ No direct
0PS_ACTVTY_ATTRNetwork ActivityNo standard CDS❌ Missing
0PS_RELATIONSHPRelationshipsNo standard CDS❌ Missing

PS Costs/Actuals Extractors

BW Extractor Description CDS View Option Status

0PS_C03_1Plan Costs WBSI_ProjctPlannedCostsByPeriod⚠️ Partial
0PS_C03_2Actual Costs WBSI_ActualCostsByWBSElement⚠️ Partial
0CO_OM_WBS_6WBS CommitmentsI_CommitmentItemHierarchy❌ Workaround
0CO_OM_CCA_9Cost Centers (PS related)I_CostCenterCost⚠️ Partial
0PS_C04_1Plan Costs NetworkNo standard CDS❌ Missing

PS Progress Extractors

BW Extractor Description CDS View Option Status

0PS_C07_1Milestone Trend AnalysisNo standard CDS❌ Missing
0PS_C08Project ProgressNo standard CDS❌ Missing

QM Extractors vs CDS View Mapping

BW Extractor Description CDS View Option Status

0QM_CONTROLQM Control DataNo standard CDS❌ Missing
0QM_QMEL_1Quality NotificationsI_QualityNotification⚠️ Partial
0QM_CHARACTInspection CharacteristicsNo standard CDS❌ Missing
0QM_LOT_1Inspection LotsI_InspectionLot⚠️ Partial
0QM_RESULT_1Inspection ResultsI_InspectionCharacteristic⚠️ Partial
0QM_CERTIF_1Quality CertificatesNo standard CDS❌ Missing

Practical Solutions

Option 1: Custom CDS Views on Top of Standard Tables

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_PS_WBS_EXTR'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'PS WBS Element Extraction View'
@Analytics.dataExtract: true  -- Enable for Datasphere RF
@VDM.viewType: #BASIC

-- Delta handling
@Analytics.dataCategory: #FACT
@Consumption.dbHints: 'NO_RANGE_PARTITIONING'

define view ZCDS_PS_WBS_EXTRACTION
  as select from prps  -- WBS Element Master
    inner join proj on proj.pspnr = prps.psphi
    left outer join prte on prte.posnr = prps.pspnr
  
  association [0..1] to I_ProjectDefinition 
    as _ProjectDefinition 
    on _ProjectDefinition.ProjectDefinitionInternalID = prps.psphi

{
  -- Key fields
  prps.pspnr                    as WBSElementInternalID,
  prps.posid                    as WBSElement,
  prps.post1                    as WBSDescription,
  
  -- Project reference
  proj.pspnr                    as ProjectDefinitionInternalID,
  proj.pspid                    as ProjectDefinitionID,
  
  -- Control data
  prps.psphi                    as TopWBSElement,
  prps.stufe                    as HierarchyLevel,
  prps.pbukr                    as CompanyCode,
  prps.prctr                    as ProfitCenter,
  prps.kostl                    as CostCenter,
  
  -- Dates
  prps.fdat                     as ProjectStartDate,
  prps.idat                     as ProjectFinishDate,
  prte.mssst                    as ActualStartDate,
  prte.msest                    as ActualFinishDate,
  
  -- Status
  prps.usr00                    as SystemStatus,
  prps.sttxt                    as UserStatus,
  
  -- Change tracking for delta
  prps.aedat                    as LastChangedDate,
  
  -- Associations
  _ProjectDefinition
}
where prps.mandt = $session.client

Option 2: SLT (SAP Landscape Transformation) Replication

Architecture:
S4 HANA Tables (PRPS, PROJ, etc.)
        ↓
    SLT Replication
        ↓
SAP Datasphere (via SLT Connector)
        ↓
Datasphere Views/Analytic Models

Best for: Real-time replication without CDS dependency

Option 3: ODP Framework via Custom DataSources

" Register custom ODP DataSource for PS
" This allows Datasphere to consume via ODP connector

CLASS zcl_ps_odp_provider IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_rodps_provider~get_data.
    " Extract from PRPS, PROJ tables
    " Implement full/delta logic
    " Register with ODP framework
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDMETHOD.

Option 4: Datasphere Data Flow + RFC/BAPI

Datasphere Data Flow:
  Source: RFC Function Module (custom)
         → BAPI_PROJECT_GETINFO
         → Custom FM reading PRPS/PROJ
  Target: Datasphere Persistence Layer
  Schedule: Periodic (no real-time delta)

Recommended Architecture for PS/QM Gap

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           S/4 HANA System               │
│                                         │
│  ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐  │
│  │ PS/QM Tables│   │ Standard CDS    │  │
│  │ PRPS,PROJ   │   │ Where available │  │
│  │ QMEL,QALS   │   │                 │  │
│  └──────┬──────┘   └────────┬────────┘  │
│         │                   │           │
│  ┌──────▼──────────────────▼────────┐   │
│  │     Custom CDS Views             │   │
│  │  @Analytics.dataExtract: true    │   │
│  │  with proper delta handling      │   │
│  └──────────────────┬───────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────┼───────────────────┘
                      │
            ┌─────────▼──────────┐
            │  Replication Flow  │
            │  (Datasphere)      │
            │  Initial + Delta   │
            └─────────┬──────────┘
                      │
            ┌─────────▼──────────┐
            │  Datasphere        │
            │  Persistence Layer │
            │  Local Tables      │
            └────────────────────┘

Key Tables for Custom CDS Views (PS)

Table Content

PROJProject Definition Header
PRPSWBS Element Master
PRTEProject Scheduling
AUFKOrder Master (Networks)
AFVCNetwork Activities
AFVVActivity Details
RPSCOProject Cost Report
COSPCost Totals External
COSSCost Totals Internal
BPGEBudget
BPJAAnnual Budget

Key Tables for Custom CDS Views (QM)

Table Content

QMELQuality Notifications
QMMANotification Items
QALSInspection Lots
QAVEUsage Decision
QASRResults Records
QAMVInspection Characteristics

Delta Strategy for Custom CDS Views

-- Option A: Timestamp-based delta
where prps.aedat >= $parameters.p_from_date

-- Option B: Using CDPOS/CDHDR change documents  
define view ZCDS_PS_WBS_DELTA as
  select from prps
    inner join cdhdr on cdhdr.objectid = prps.posid
                     and cdhdr.objectclas = 'PROJ'
  where cdhdr.udate >= $parameters.p_delta_date

-- Option C: Triggering via DB trigger → custom delta table
-- Most reliable but requires ABAP development

Summary Recommendation

Scenario Recommended Approach

S/4 Cloud (BTP)Await SAP roadmap + Custom CDS
S/4 On-Prem with SLTSLT → Datasphere direct
S/4 On-Prem without SLTCustom CDS + Datasphere RF
Near-real-time neededSLT or Custom ODP
Batch/daily loads OKCustom CDS + Replication Flow

entry.title: Customer-Managed Data Products in SAP BDC: What Really Happens When Your Source Is a Datasphere VIEW
entry.content:
Imagine you are a data engineer at a mid-size enterprise running SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition. Your company has just subscribed to SAP Business Data Cloud. The pitch was compelling: bring your SAP data into Databricks with zero copy, no ETL pipelines, no data duplication. You have an existing Datasphere implementation where your team already built a clean, business-ready General Ledger Account master data VIEW. It joins the SKA1 chart-of-accounts table and the SKAT text table, filters to English language, and projects the analytical columns your finance team needs. It took weeks to get right. You want to publish that as a Customer-Managed Data Product and share it to your Enterprise Databricks workspace.

You follow the documented steps. You create a Datasphere HDLF (HANA Data Lake Files) space. You try to point the CMDP directly at your VIEW. And then you discover something nobody told you upfront: you cannot directly share a Datasphere HANA relational VIEW as a Delta-shareable data product. The VIEW must first be materialized into a physical HANA local table. That HANA local table must then be exported to an HDLF Delta Lake file via a Transformation Flow. Only from that HDLF Delta Lake file can the CMDP be published and shared to Databricks via Delta Sharing.

Two physical copies. You thought there would be zero.

Your first reaction is frustration. Your second is a question: did SAP misrepresent the zero-copy architecture? The answer is no, but the explanation requires a level of precision that the marketing material does not always provide. This post provides that precision.

Part 1: The Foundations: What Delta Sharing Actually Guarantees

Before diagnosing the architecture, you need to understand exactly what Delta Sharing is and what its zero-copy promise covers, because this is where the misalignment between expectation and reality originates.

Delta Sharing is an open protocol (originally developed by Databricks, now vendor-neutral) for governed, read-access sharing of data stored in Delta Lake format from a provider to a consumer, without the consumer needing to import or duplicate that data. Delta Lake format means Parquet columnar data files plus a transaction log: a folder of JSON files recording every change operation, stored in a cloud object store (Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2, or Google Cloud Storage depending on your hyperscaler).

The zero-copy guarantee that Delta Sharing makes is specifically and exclusively at the consumer boundary. When Databricks reads a Delta-shared table, the sequence at the protocol level works as follows. Databricks authenticates to the SAP BDC Delta Sharing server using mutual TLS (mTLS, meaning both sides exchange certificates to prove identity) and OpenID Connect (OIDC) for token-based authorization. The server issues a time-limited, pre-signed URL pointing directly to the Parquet files in the HDLF object store. Databricks reads those files directly from HDLF over HTTPS. No data enters Databricks storage. The URL expires. The next query gets fresh URLs. No copy is ever created on the Databricks side.

This is real. It works exactly as described. Zero copy at the consumer boundary is a genuine architectural guarantee that SAP BDC delivers today.

What Delta Sharing has never guaranteed (not in SAP BDC, not in Databricks, not on any platform from any vendor) is the elimination of producer-side preparation steps. Delta Sharing can only share data that already exists in Delta Lake format in an object store. If your data lives in a relational table, a view, a CSV, or any other format, it must be converted to Parquet and Delta Log before Delta Sharing can reference it. That conversion is not duplication in any meaningful business sense. It is a format transformation required by the protocol itself. This same requirement exists on AWS, Azure, GCP, Databricks native, Snowflake, and every other platform implementing Delta Sharing. SAP is not the exception here. SAP is the standard.

The producer-side storage pattern (one copy, or two) depends entirely on a separate decision: where your merge and join logic lives. That is what the rest of this post addresses.

Part 2: The Two HDLF Object Stores in SAP BDC

Before explaining the two architecture options, you need a clear mental model of where data physically lives in SAP BDC. There are two distinct HDLF object store instances and confusing them is a common source of misunderstanding.

The Foundation Services HDLF (FOS) is managed entirely by SAP. It stores SAP-Managed Data Products (the out-of-the-box data products for S/4HANA Finance, Procurement, Sales, HR, and so on). When you activate an Intelligent Application in the BDC Cockpit, SAP automatically populates the relevant data products in the FOS layer. Customers have no direct access to this layer. SAP handles extraction, replication, transformation, and publication invisibly.

The Datasphere HDLF Object Store is managed by the customer within their Datasphere tenant. This is where Customer-Managed Data Products live. Customers configure, populate, transform, and govern this space themselves. It is backed by the same hyperscaler object storage technology as the FOS layer. The difference is ownership, access, and responsibility.

Customer-Managed Data Products can only be published from the Datasphere HDLF object store. They cannot be published directly from the HANA relational space. This is the design constraint that determines everything about the architecture options below.

Part 3: The Real-World Use Case: GL Account Master Data from S/4HANA

The worked example throughout this post is General Ledger Account master data from SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE), one of the most common CMDP scenarios in enterprise analytics implementations.

In S/4HANA, GL Account master data is stored across two key ABAP tables:

  • SKA1: The chart of accounts segment, holding account number, account type, account group, P&L classification, and balance sheet flag. One record per account per chart of accounts.
  • SKAT: The text segment, holding account short text and long text per language key. Multiple records per account.

A business-ready GL Account master data product for Databricks needs to join SKA1 and SKAT on account number and chart of accounts key, filter to English language, and project the meaningful analytical attributes into a flat, single-row-per-account dataset.

This join is the "merge" that drives the architecture decision. Where you perform it determines whether you end up with one physical copy or two on the SAP side.

Part 4: Option A: Upstream Merge (One Physical Copy)

The core principle: Push the join logic upstream into S/4HANA as a CDS view. By the time data leaves S/4HANA and arrives in Datasphere, it is already a single, merged, business-ready dataset. Datasphere's Replication Flow lands this dataset directly into an HDLF File Space as a Delta Lake local table. One physical copy. No HANA relational space involved at any point.

This option applies to both S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE) and Public Cloud Edition (CE).

On Private Cloud Edition, your ABAP team builds an extraction-enabled, CDC-capable custom CDS view (for example, ZI_GL_ACCOUNT_MASTER) that performs the SKA1-SKAT join directly in S/4HANA. The view carries the annotation @analytics.dataextraction.enabled: true, making it available in the CDS_EXTRACTION container in Datasphere's Replication Flow. Connectivity from Datasphere to S/4HANA on-premises runs via SAP Cloud Connector, a lightweight outbound-only tunnel that requires no inbound firewall rule changes.

On Public Cloud Edition, the same pattern applies. Custom CDS views are created via the Custom CDS View tile in Fiori Launchpad with a YY1_ prefix. No ABAP IDE or developer access is required. Connectivity is a direct HTTPS API call using the ABAP SQL Service communication arrangement, with no Cloud Connector needed.

The data flow for Option A:

  1. S/4HANA ABAP Layer: Custom CDS view ZI_GL_ACCOUNT_MASTER joins SKA1 and SKAT, filters to language EN, selects analytical columns. Extraction and CDC annotations are activated on the view.
  2. Replication Flow (Datasphere): Reads ZI_GL_ACCOUNT_MASTER from the CDS_EXTRACTION container. The target is an HDLF File Space (Storage Type: SAP HANA Data Lake Files). Data lands in the HDLF inbound buffer.
  3. Merge Task: Runs on a schedule to consolidate the inbound buffer into the HDLF local table (file), creating the Parquet files and Delta transaction log. This is the one and only physical copy on the SAP side.
  4. Transformation Flow (optional): If additional silver layer logic is needed (inactive account filtering, currency enrichment, language fallback), a Spark-based Transformation Flow applies it and writes to a Silver HDLF local table. Still effectively one net dataset if Bronze is transient.
  5. CMDP Publication: A Data Provider Profile with Formations visibility is created. A Customer-Managed Data Product is defined pointing to the HDLF local table as its Product Artifact. The ORD (Open Resource Discovery) metadata descriptor is published to the BDC catalog.
  6. Delta Sharing to Enterprise Databricks: BDC Connect (generally available since October 2025) establishes the two-sided trust between BDC and the customer's existing Databricks workspace. mTLS and OIDC handle authentication. The BDC Delta Sharing server issues signed URLs. Databricks reads Parquet files directly from HDLF over HTTPS. Zero copy at the consumer boundary.

When to choose Option A: New CMDP implementations starting from scratch where the ABAP team can build or extend a CDS view in S/4HANA. High-volume, high-frequency datasets where eliminating the HANA in-memory footprint has meaningful cost impact. Any scenario where you want the leanest possible producer-side architecture.

Part 5: Option B: Datasphere View Merge (Two Physical Copies)

The core principle: Build the join logic as a VIEW inside Datasphere's HANA relational space. This VIEW must be materialized into a physical HANA local table before a Transformation Flow can read it and produce the HDLF Delta Lake file. Two physical copies result on the SAP side: one in HANA relational storage, one in HDLF object storage.

This is the required path when merge logic already lives in a Datasphere HANA VIEW, as it does in most mature Datasphere deployments.

The technical reason this two-copy pattern exists is straightforward: a Datasphere HANA VIEW is a virtual object. It is a saved query definition. It contains no data. A Transformation Flow requires a physical source to read from. The VIEW must therefore be materialized into a physical HANA local table first. Additionally, Replication Flows in Datasphere are designed to read from external source systems (S/4HANA, other ABAP sources, cloud databases). They cannot use a Datasphere HANA VIEW or local table as their source. Directly writing a Datasphere VIEW result to HDLF without materialization is not a supported path today.

This behavior is not unique to SAP. In Oracle, SQL Server, Snowflake, and every relational database system on earth, a view is a virtual object. Materializing before exporting is the standard pattern everywhere.

The data flow for Option B:

  1. S/4HANA ABAP Layer: SKA1 and SKAT are individually extraction-enabled CDS views. Two Replication Flows extract each table separately into Datasphere.
  2. Datasphere HANA Relational Space (Copy 1): SKA1 and SKAT land as separate HANA local tables. This is the first physical copy of the data on the SAP side.
  3. Datasphere HANA VIEW (virtual, no additional copy): A graphical or SQL VIEW in Datasphere joins the two local tables on account number and chart of accounts key, filters to language EN, and projects the analytical columns. This VIEW holds no data.
  4. Materialization to HANA Local Table: The VIEW output persisted into a new physical HANA local table via a Data Flow or view persistency, producing the merged, flat, single-row-per-account dataset in HANA. Still part of Copy 1 (the HANA relational layer).
  5. Transformation Flow to HDLF (Copy 2): A Transformation Flow reads the materialized HANA local table and writes the result as a Delta Lake file (Parquet and transaction log) into the Datasphere HDLF File Space. This is the second physical copy on the SAP side.
  6. CMDP Publication: Same as Option A. Data Provider Profile, CMDP definition, ORD descriptor, BDC catalog publication.
  7. Delta Sharing to Enterprise Databricks: Identical to Option A from this point. BDC Connect, mTLS and OIDC, signed URLs, direct Parquet reads from HDLF. Zero copy at the consumer boundary.

When to choose Option B: When merge and join logic already exists as a Datasphere HANA VIEW and rebuilding it upstream is not justified by the business case. Low-to-medium volume reference data (GL Account master, Cost Center master, Profit Center master) where the HANA memory cost of the materialized table is negligible. When the Datasphere VIEW contains complex business logic (multi-table joins, CASE expressions, aggregations) that would require significant effort to replicate in a CDS view or Spark Transformation Flow.

Part 6: Option A vs. Option B (The Decision Table)

Part 7: How Delta Sharing Actually Transits Data to Enterprise Databricks

For completeness, here is the exact connectivity and transit story for Enterprise Databricks (what SAP calls the "brownfield" scenario, meaning the customer's own independently licensed Databricks workspace, not the SAP Databricks bundled inside BDC). This is the part customers ask about most in architecture reviews.

Connecting BDC to Enterprise Databricks via BDC Connect:

BDC Connect became generally available for all three hyperscale's in October 2025. The connection establishment is a one-time, two-sided handshake:

  1. The Databricks workspace admin generates a connection identifier in Databricks (Data Ingestion > SAP Business Data Cloud tile) and shares it with the SAP BDC admin.
  2. The SAP BDC admin uses that identifier to create a Third Party Connection in BDC and generates an invitation link.
  3. The Databricks admin completes the connection using that invitation link. The confirmation message reads: "Connection to SAP Business Data Cloud was successful."
  4. BDC is now registered as an authorized Delta Sharing provider in the Databricks Unity Catalog. The connection is bidirectional.

The read path (every time Databricks queries a shared table):

  1. Databricks authenticates to the SAP BDC Delta Sharing server using mTLS (mutual Transport Layer Security, where both sides present certificates) and OIDC (OpenID Connect for token-based identity verification).
  2. The Delta Sharing server validates entitlements against the governance policy, covering which data product, which recipient, and which columns or partitions are authorized.
  3. The server issues time-limited pre-signed URLs pointing directly to the specific Parquet files in the HDLF object store that constitute the requested table or delta increment.
  4. Databricks reads those Parquet files directly from HDLF (S3, ADLS Gen2, or GCS) over HTTPS. Data travels encrypted in transit. No SAP middleware sits in this read path.
  5. The signed URLs expire. No persistent copy of the data is created in Databricks storage.

This is zero copy in operational terms. The Parquet files never leave the HDLF object store. Whether those Parquet files were produced via Option A (one copy on the SAP side) or Option B (two copies on the SAP side) is entirely irrelevant to this read path. The consumer experience is identical either way.

Part 8: The Reverse Flow: What Happens When Databricks Sends Enriched Data Back to BDC

A question that comes up in almost every customer architecture discussion: when Databricks runs ML models or analytics on top of the CMDP and sends results back to BDC, does it update the original CMDP? Or is it a new data product?

The answer is unambiguous. It is always a brand new, separate Derived Data Product. The original CMDP is never modified, overwritten, or versioned by the reverse flow.

The data scientist publishes the enriched dataset (say, a GL Account clustering result produced by an ML model) back to BDC using the BDC Connect SDK in a Databricks notebook. Three SDK calls are made in sequence: bdc_connect_client.create_or_update_share() registers the share with its ORD metadata, bdc_connect_client.create_or_update_share_csn() defines the data schema using the CSN (Core Schema Notation) format, and bdc_connect_client.publish_data_product() makes the data product discoverable in the BDC catalog. The new Derived Data Product appears in the BDC Cockpit Catalog and Marketplace as a distinct, independently governed entry, discoverable by SAP Analytics Cloud reports, Datasphere models, and other data products.

The original GL Account master CMDP remains exactly as it was: unchanged, unaware, still serving as a source for other consumers. The Derived Data Product and the original CMDP coexist in the BDC catalog as independent assets with their own lineage, their own governance policies, and their own update schedules. This composability is the foundation of SAP's data product strategy and the direction that Data Product Studio (currently in preview, maturing through 2026) is designed to operationalize at low-code scale.

Closing: The Definitive Framing

The most precise way to describe the SAP BDC zero-copy architecture for Customer-Managed Data Products is this:

SAP BDC delivers genuine zero-copy data sharing to Enterprise Databricks at the consumer boundary via the Delta Sharing protocol. The producer-side pipeline (one physical copy in Option A, two in Option B) prepares the data in Delta Lake format as required by the Delta Sharing protocol. That preparation is not duplication. It is format conversion. It is architecturally equivalent to what every other vendor implementing Delta Sharing requires on every platform, without exception.

The choice between Option A and Option B is not a choice between correct and incorrect architecture. It is a design decision driven by where your merge logic lives and what trade-offs make sense for your specific data product, your S/4HANA deployment type, and your existing Datasphere investment. Both options are fully supported by SAP today. Both deliver genuine zero copy to Databricks. The difference is purely in the producer-side storage footprint and the location of your business logic.

If you are an architect, a CSM, or a customer who has been confused by this, I hope this post has made the picture clear. If your team is navigating the CMDP journey on SAP BDC right now, whether on Private Cloud or Public Cloud, whether starting fresh or working with an existing Datasphere implementation, feel free to reach out in the comments or directly.

Data and Analytics SAP Business Data Cloud RISE with SAP SAP S/4HANA SAP Datasphere SAP S/4HANA SAP Databricks ABAP Development SAP BTP ABAP environment

entry.title: Update SAP standard field cost center in PO line item using ME21N /ME22N
entry.content:
Hi All,

I need to update the SAP standard Cost Center field at the PO line item level.

To achieve this, I implemented the BAdI ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST and used the method PROCESS_ACCOUNT. The implementation is working fine for standard Purchase Orders, and I am able to populate the Cost Center successfully.

However, the same logic is not working in the case of Service Purchase Orders.

Below is the code I used to populate the Cost Center field.

DATA : ls_accounting     TYPE mepoaccounting,
           ls_get_accounting TYPE purchase_order_accounting.
    ls_accounting = im_account->get_data( ).
    SELECT SINGLE cycle FROM coas INTO @DATA(lv_cycle) WHERE aufnr = @LS_accounting-aufnr.
    IF sy-subrc = 0 AND lv_cycle IS NOT INITIAL.
      ls_accounting-kostl = lv_cycle.
    ELSE.
      SELECT SINGLE kostv FROM coas INTO @DATA(lv_kostv) WHERE aufnr = @LS_accounting-aufnr.
      ls_accounting-kostl =  lv_kostv.
    ENDIF.
    im_account->set_data( ls_accounting ).
    im_account->invalidate( ).

 Below is the service PO not working for this.

ABAP Development #

ISNULL() does not check if measure value is null. It checks if a condition evaluates to null, You can adjust your formula to be something like below.

IF(ABS([#VAL FATT AC] )>=0 ,[#VAL FATT AC] , 0)

 

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: CPI JMS QUEUE CORRELATION ID MANUEL SET
entry.content:
Hi,

You cannot directly set the JMSCorrelationID in JMS Sender adapter.  It is a read-only parameter in the sender adapter.  However, if you want to set a custom JMSCorrelationID, you can set it to the Receiver adapter flow using (message.setHeader("JMSCorrelationID", ***)) and then read it in the next Sender adapter step.

SAP_MplCorrelationId- is the MessageProcessingLog ID set by CPI for tracking. It is a read only value and cannot be modified. It is different from JMSCorrelationID.

entry.title: Re: Understanding HANA memory related license utilization in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hi @Claudia_Semmler, that's a great text, thanks for sharing!

I actually came here because I'm looking for ways to monitor license usage, so first they shared note 1704499 and then now I also came across note 1969700, that has the 'HANA_License_LicenseUsage' query.

So even after some reading and investigation on different tables, I was still a bit confused, but what I figured is the following, for ways of monitoring license usage:

  • Get license limit in GB:
    • M_LICENSE.PRODUCT_USAGE
    • M.LICENSES.PRODUCT_USAGE (here, you might want to filter for GLAS_APPLICATION_ID = '000673')
  • Get maximum memory HANA is allowed to allocate from the OS in bytes
    • M_HOST_RESOURCE_UTILIZATION.ALLOCATION_LIMIT
  • Get license usage in GB 
    • M_LICENSE_USAGE_HISTORY.PRODUCT_USAGE
    • M_LICENSE.PRODUCT_USAGE (historical peak)
    • M_LICENSE_MEASUREMENTS.VALUE (WHERE GLAS_APPLICATION_ID = '000673')

And then it should make sense to set the allocation limit to a value close to the license limit, right? I believe that if the allocation limit is set to the same value as the license limit, then it would never be exceeded. Also it's worth mentioning that even if the limit has changed, no table will show the historical value for that, only for the usage.

Is that the correct understanding from your experience?

entry.title: bgPF is replacing IDoc for Intercompany Payables Posting in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
This blog is about Background Processing Framework (bgPF) Is Replacing IDoc for Intercompany Payables Posting in S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition and it describes What’s changing, who’s impacted, and how to adopt safely with 2602.2 and beyond.

SAP is transitioning intercompany payables posting for “classic” intercompany invoices from the legacy IDoc-based mechanism to the background processing framework (bgPF). Starting with release 2602.2 in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, customers can activate a new feature that enables bgPF to automatically post payables to the selling company’s Accounts Payable. Over time, bgPF will become the default and ultimately the system standard, fully replacing IDocs for this use case. The bgPF solution is easier to configure, more flexible, and helps you adjust to future business requirements.

  • Short term (2602.2): Feature is opt‑in via Activate Features.
  • Upcoming: Feature will be activated by default (with an option to deactivate).
  • Future: bgPF becomes standard; IDoc-based posting retired.

Important: Once you activate the feature, the system can no longer post payables via IDoc. Post any unposted payables before activation to avoid stranding documents mid-flow.

 What’s changing

As of today, the Intercompany payables posting can run via IDoc. With the 2602.2, bgPF can be used to automatically post payables to the selling company’s AP. The Activation of this feature named Payables Posting for Classic Intercompany Invoice via bgPF with technical id S4HC_SDBIL_ICO_IDOC_REMOVAL is controlled in the Activate Features app and is not automatic. The bgPF option will first become the default (with an opt‑out), and later the only available mechanism, replacing the IDoc-based solution entirely.

 Impacted scope items

The change affects intercompany processing in Order-to-Cash and Returns scenarios:

  • Intercompany Sales Order Processing – Domestic (1HO)
  • Intercompany Sales Order Processing – International (1MX)
  • Extended Intercompany Processes for Sales (1WO)
  • Intercompany Process for Customer Returns (2F4)
  • Intercompany Billing for Cross‑Company Cost Accounting Postings (4AN)

Release of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

bgPF Solution

IDoc Solution

2602 to 2608.4

Available (opt-in)

System default

2702

System default (opt-out available)

Available (via feature deactivation)

<Subsequent release>

(for example, 2708 or 2802)

Only solution

Removed from system

 Activation guidance (2602.2)

Because the feature is opt‑in at this stage, the activation of the feature has to be done manually.

Prerequisites:

  • We recommend closing out unposted payables created via IDoc by posting the corresponding intercompany invoice to financial accounting.

Activation steps:

1. Activate the feature in the Activate Features activity in Implementation activities app:

    • Feature name: Payables Posting for Classic Intercompany Invoice via bgPF
    • Technical ID: S4HC_SDBIL_ICO_IDOC_REMOVAL

2. Setup Output determination:

    • Define the business rules for output determination in the Output Parameter Determination app which will enable the use of output channel EDI instead of output channel IDOC.             

3. Enable monitoring of bgPF messages:

    • In order to enable monitoring of bgPF messages by business users, the Assign Recipients to Users app should be used to assign the recipient name EDI_FIN_IC_AP_IN_RECIPIENT to the users. For setup instructions, please check the scope item 1HO.

Rollback/opt‑out:

  • In an upcoming release, the feature will be activated by default with an option to deactivate. Use that opt‑out only if a critical issue is discovered and log a corrective action plan. Note that the long‑term direction is bgPF standardization.

 Recommendations

  • After activation, payables can no longer be posted via IDoc for the affected scope.
  • Post all outstanding IDoc-triggered payables before activation to prevent mixed-mode exceptions.
  • Recommend complete testing of the end-to-end process in your quality system before enabling the feature in production system.

entry.title: 1H 2026 Features: Your People Profile Just Got a Major Onboarding Upgrade: A Deeper Dive
entry.content:
One Place for Every Document That Matters

I've been waiting to announce and publish this one for a while, because it's a significant and major integration between Onboarding and Employee Central's Latest People Profile, and something customers have been asking for a long time. 

At long last: Onboarding-related documents (and attachments) will now show on Employee Central's Latest People Profile, aka, ECT PPX!

 

One Place for Every Document That Matters

One of the most anticipated additions to the Latest People Profile is the new Documents section — a centralized hub where employees can find every important document tied to their employment journey, all in one place. This includes Onboarding documents. 

No more hunting through separate systems or workflows. Whether it's onboarding compliance forms, a completed Form I-9, or other HR-critical documents, they now surface directly within the employee's own profile. The experience is role-aware, too: managers with the appropriate permissions can see the documents they're authorized to view, while employees always have full access to their own records.

Availability: Early Adoption in 1H 2026 — General Availability planned for 2H 2026.

Getting set up is straightforward. Administrators can configure the Documents section visibility through Configure Latest People Profile, with options to make it universally accessible or controlled through Role-Based Permissions (RBP) for fine-grained access management.

 

 

The Bigger Picture

These enhancements reflect a clear vision: the People Profile should be the definitive source of truth for everything related to an employee — from the moment they begin onboarding through every milestone in their career. With documents, compliance, talent data, and onboarding status all converging in one experience, HR teams gain efficiency, employees gain transparency, and organizations gain confidence in their data.

Stay tuned — many of these capabilities are in Early Adoption now, with General Availability coming in 2H 2026. We encourage customers to explore the Early Adoption program and experience these features firsthand.

For more on what's new in 1H 2026, visit the SAP SuccessFactors What's New Viewer.

entry.title: Re: SFTP post-processing delete mode in poll enricher doe...
entry.content:
Hi,
SFTP sender adapter "Delete" option  works only for the main file, not for the files fetched via Poll Enricher.  Hence only the first file gets deleted, and the remaining files fetched via PollEnricher remains as-is.

What you can try doing is - While processing each file in the loop, capture the file name and move it to an archive location via another SFTP receiver.

entry.title: The Agentic Shift in SAP Fieldglass: From “Helpful AI” to Work that Drives Itself
entry.content:
The Agentic Shift in SAP Fieldglass: From “Helpful AI” to Work that Drives Itself

The biggest delays in external workforce and services procurement rarely come from the system itself — they come from everything around it. Requirements are unclear, key information is missing, approvals stall, onboarding documents arrive late, suppliers go back and forth, and teams end up redoing work that should have been right the first time.

That’s why the latest evolution of agentic AI in SAP Fieldglass matters. It’s not about adding another AI feature. It’s about changing how work progresses: shifting beyond isolated moments of assistance toward agentic experiences that help prepare, validate, and move work forward across lifecycle steps while keeping people in control of the decisions that matter most.

Before you can trust agents in a real contingent or procurement environment, you need strong fundamentals, including: consistent inputs, reliable guidance, and guardrails that respect templates, permissions, and program rules.

That’s exactly what SAP Fieldglass has been investing in with capabilities such as:

  • Joule a conversational experience layer that makes AI practical day to day by allowing you to interact with the system in natural language.
  • Faster creation of contingent and statement of work (SOW) content (such as job/SOW description generation and translation, template selection, worker role recommendation) to reduce rework and improve quality before a request even reaches suppliers.
  • Skills and candidate intelligence (such as resume assessment and skills highlighting) to help reviewers focus on job‑relevant evidence more quickly.

These aren’t nice extras. They’re the building blocks that make agentic workflows scalable and trustworthy in production.

Why does this matter in the agentic era?

In SAP Fieldglass terms, it means the platform does more of the heavy lifting within your governance model. Practically, it looks like turning intent into a clean draft that’s aligned to configured templates and rules and then routing it through the normal approvals rather than bouncing between teams. Just as important: Fieldglass’s agent vision is explicit about keeping humans in the loop for every critical decision to support fairness and transparency.

How will you benefit?

1) Faster, cleaner services procurement

On the services side, customers consistently ask for the same thing: make the SOW process faster, clearer, and easier to manage without losing control. The most natural starting point is exactly where the highest friction occurs today—creation, change, and insight.

That’s why the first three SOW agents are so practical and easy to understand:

  • SOW Creation Agent: Turns intent (and supporting documents where relevant) into a structured, review-ready SOW draft aligned to templates and rules. 
  • SOW Change Management Agent: Supports controlled change by capturing revisions with a clear change record and impact visibility, routed through approvals.
  • SOW Insights Agent: Converts questions into KPIs and surfaces risks early with evidence, so teams can act before issues become disputes or delays.

The value here is not “AI wrote some paragraphs." The value is less ambiguity, fewer disputes, faster approvals, and stronger governance because the work stays structured and traceable through the lifecycle.

2) Accelerated hiring throughput without sacrificing control

In contingent programs, upstream quality determines downstream speed. If a posting is unclear or incomplete, suppliers respond with noise, reviewers burn time sorting it, and start dates slip because everyone is forced into avoidable rework.

That’s exactly where contingent workforce agents start to earn their keep. “AI help” stops being a side panel of suggestions and starts acting more like guided execution for the requisition itself—turning a hiring manager’s simple intent into a draft that’s ready for review while also connecting policy and skills data across the journey.

In practice, that means recommending the best‑fit posting template, enforcing the user’s scope (sites, cost objects, suppliers), pre-filling required fields and qualifications, and even proposing supplier distribution rules. Then everything get handed back to the user for a clear human-in-the-loop review before submission into the normal approval workflow.

3) Governance that scales (autonomy with guardrails)

In enterprise procurement, adoption dies the moment users think AI will bypass controls. SAP Fieldglass tackles that head-on with explicit guardrails: identity and access checks, permission enforcement, validation before completion, user control (draft vs submit), and traceability (what changed and why).

That’s what makes agentic experiences deployable. AI accelerates execution, but governance stays intact.

Joule: The experience layer that makes this usable day to day

A major part of making AI “stick” is not forcing users into a new tool, but rather meeting them where they already work. With Joule as the conversational layer, users can access help and guidance, find information faster, and interact more naturally with what they need to do.

Small reductions in friction—finding the right report, understanding the next step, getting guidance for completion—add up quickly in high-volume programs.

Conclusion

The agentic shift isn’t a single release or feature. It’s a new operating model for external workforce and services work, where SAP Fieldglass reduces friction throughout your processes and policies and teams spend less time chasing information and more time making the decisions that matter. Looking ahead, the biggest gains will come as these experiences connect across steps — cleaner drafts upstream, earlier validation of gaps and exceptions, smoother progression through approvals, and more consistent outcomes at scale — all while preserving the human checkpoints and audit trail that real effectives programs depend on.

Figure – SOW Creation Agent

entry.title: Re: Dropping scheduled documents from Webi to SharePoint...
entry.content:
You’re correct—Microsoft’s changes to the SharePoint Online APIs have made the old direct scheduling approach from Web Intelligence much more challenging.

One option you may want to look at is InfoBurst (from InfoSol), which is specifically designed to handle report bursting and distribution for SAP BusinessObjects (and two other leading analytics platfoms).

With InfoBurst, you can:

  • Schedule and distribute Crystal Reports and WebI reports independently of the native BI scheduler
  • Deliver outputs directly to SharePoint Online using modern authentication (OAuth / Microsoft Graph)
  • Avoid reliance on deprecated APIs or legacy connections
  • Centralize distribution logic instead of maintaining multiple workarounds

Compared to something like SkySync, it’s typically more focused on the BI use case, so you don’t need as much cross-team involvement or file-sync orchestration—it’s handled as part of the report distribution process.

It also gives you additional flexibility like:

  • Bursting reports to different recipients/locations
  • Multi-format delivery (PDF, Excel, etc.)
  • Integration with other endpoints (email, Teams, OneDrive, Box, Dropbox, etc.)

If your main goal is to restore automated delivery to SharePoint Online without heavy reengineering, this approach tends to be much more manageable.

I even tried to transport just a Time Type Group that does not have the Attendance Quota type, but am still getting the error. BTW, the field is blank in all time type groups.

entry.title: Re: Enable Payment Approval Process Before Posting Paymen...
entry.content:
Dear Nandini,

There are two prerequisite of activating payment approval workflow:

  • Activation of the Scope Advanced Cash Operations (J78) is required.
  • In the configuration activity Define Basic Settings (SSCUI ID 101025) cash scope must be selected as Full Scope.

Please kindly review the KBA 3731235 - Enabling approval workflow for payment runs in Manage Automatic Payments app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me for more details.

Video demonstration: Workflow for Payment Approval - SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Similar question: Solved: Payment approval workflow - SAP Community.

I hope this helps.

Best regards,
Gordon

entry.title: How to make story NOT planning enabled by default
entry.content:
Stories created by users on a planning model are set to planning enabled by default.

We have a requirement where a Team of users need the ability to create reporting stories on a planning model. The Team members that are not Planning users cannot publish any data in these stories as expected, however users that have planning roles are able to publish data in the user created stories, as the story is "Planning Enabled" when created. We are looking for a way to ensure the user created stories on a planning model are not able to be used to enter data by a planning user. The planning users do enter data in “authorised” planning stories as part of the planning process.

entry.title: Introducing Time Side Navigation in SAP SuccessFactors Time Management
entry.content:
1. The Challenge: Finding Time Management Features Shouldn't Be Hard

If you've ever struggled to find your timesheet, time-off request, or team absence calendar in SAP SuccessFactors, you're not alone. Time Management is one of the most-used areas of the SuccessFactors suite, yet it has never had a dedicated entry point in the main navigation menu.

A UX audit surfaced a consistent set of pain points:

  • Time Management is not listed in the main menu or action dropdown — users must know where to look.
  • Employees rely on Quick Actions which may not be intuitive for new users.
  • Breadcrumbs are missing, making it difficult to understand where you are or how to navigate back.
  • Time applications such as Time Off are buried inside the People Profile, making them hard to discover.

2. Introducing the Time Side Navigation

Time Side Navigation brings a long-overdue improvement: a dedicated Time Management entry in the SAP SuccessFactors main menu, paired with an intuitive side navigation panel. This puts all time-related tasks for employees (ESS) exactly where users expect them: at the top level of the application.

Time Management will now sit alongside other first-class modules such as Recruiting, Learning, Opportunity Marketplace, and Performance & Goals, with its own second-level navigation to key tasks.

3. Key Capabilities

For Employees (ESS)

  • Quick Time Recording: Launch the Time Sheet directly from the menu — no more navigating through the People Profile.
  • Quick Absence Creation: Request time off from the main navigation in just a few clicks.
  • My Calendar: Access a personal view of your schedule and absences directly from the menu.
  • Payout Leave, Purchase Leave, Time Forms & Time Statements: All time-related self-service options accessible from one place.
  • Attendance Quota: Request attendance quota for overtime in just few clicks.

4. UX Design Decisions & Approach

The design went through a rigorous discovery and prototyping process, with multiple UX variants evaluated before arriving at the current approach. Key design decisions include:

  • Side navigation for personal data: Side navigation is most effective when employees view their own time data — the primary use case this design optimizes for.
  • Consistent horizontal tab bar: When navigating from side navigation to the next screen, a horizontal tab bar ensures a consistent experience.
  • Feature toggle controlled: The feature is delivered behind a feature toggle, allowing customers and partners to adopt at their own pace as early adopters

 

Approach

  1. For best experience, please enable this switch from company and logo settings. Also, you would need to have new to have new people profile (PPX) enabled                                                                          2. You would now see under the shell bar also a dedicated Time Management Menu (along with sub menu items). Now log in as an employee, you should see the new shell bar also called as hamburger menu  

    The visibility of sub menu items depends on RBPs. Employees need access to relevant screens such as requesting time off and timesheet access.

    3. Once you navigate from the side menu, you end up in a screen where the horizontal tab bar is shown. You can then navigate to different Time Management Transactions from the same screen

    This effectively improves productivity and efficiency. With just one click, you can navigate and perform time management tasks in one place.

5. Competitive Context

Leading HCM solutions already provide both menu-based and quick-action access to Time Management, making navigation seamless for employees. The Time Side Navigation brings SAP SuccessFactors Time Management to the same level of accessibility and intuitiveness that modern enterprise HR users expect.

6. What This Means for Customers & Partners

The Time Side Navigation delivers tangible value across three dimensions:

  • Faster task completion: Employees spend less time searching for the right screen and more time on their actual work.
  • Reduced training overhead: An intuitive, discoverable navigation reduces the learning curve for new users and cuts down on support requests.
  • Partner readiness: Partners implementing or extending SAP SuccessFactors Time Management can align configurations to a first-class navigation entry point, simplifying integration and onboarding.

7. Availability & Roadmap

  • Rollout: Feature toggle controlled in 2605 — opt-in at go-live, with General Availability planned in a subsequent release
  • Future Outlook:  Planned GA in 2611 via Company and Logo Settings. Customers can then enable this feature without depending on SAP for feature toggle activation.

8. Things to keep in mind

This feature is not available for customers that use only Leave of Absence. This requires full time off at a minimum.

9. How Can you request this as an early adopter?

This is an early adopter feature behind a feature toggle. You can request this by reaching out to us. You can reach out to us via your customer success manager, account team or directly to me via SAP community. You can drop me a message in community and we can get connected for this

Summary

This feature is a meaningful step forward in making SAP SuccessFactors Time Management more accessible, intuitive, and efficient for the employees who rely on it every day. By giving Time Management a dedicated place in the navigation alongside Recruiting, Learning, and other core modules, we are removing friction and putting time-related tasks right where they belong.

We look forward to your feedback as this feature comes to life.

entry.title: Re: How to validate Valuation Type in PR following Info R...
entry.content: With the Standard SAP behavior, the valuation type is not automatically copied from the Info Record into the Purchase Requisition (PR). SAP’s standard design expects valuation type to be determined at the material/plant level (via split valuation) or entered manually in the PR. There is no standard configuration that forces PR to inherit valuation type directly from the Info Record. If your business process requires this, you would typically need a custom enhancement (e.g., via BAdI ME_PROCESS_REQ_CUST or user exit MEREQ001) to populate the valuation type field in the PR when the Info Record is assigned.

entry.title: Re: Problem connecting with ODBC
entry.content:
@ryan_hansen,

I used the ASAN_OPTION and it worked.

 

However it looks like ASAN in general is not compatible with ASE ODBC. 😉

 

Following code:

 

[code]

int result = 0;
std::wstring query;
unsigned long versionMajor = 0, versionMinor = 0;
SQLLEN cbVersion = SQL_NTS;
SQLWCHAR version[1024];
query = L"SELECT @@version_number AS version, @@version_as_integer / 1000000 AS major, ( @@version_as_integer / 1000 - ((@@version_as_integer / 1000000)*1000) ) / 100 AS minor;";
SQLRETURN retcode = SQLAllocHandle( SQL_HANDLE_STMT, m_hdbc, &m_hstmt );
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, CONN_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
else
{
std::unique_ptr<SQLWCHAR[]> qry( new SQLWCHAR[query.length() + 2] );
memset( qry.get(), '\0', query.length() + 2 );
uc_to_str_cpy( qry.get(), query );
retcode = SQLExecDirect( m_hstmt, qry.get(), SQL_NTS );
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, STMT_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
else
{
retcode = SQLBindCol( m_hstmt, 1, SQL_C_WCHAR, &version, 1024, &cbVersion );
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, STMT_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
else
{
retcode = SQLBindCol( m_hstmt, 2, SQL_C_SLONG, &versionMajor, 0, 0 );
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, STMT_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
else
{
retcode = SQLBindCol( m_hstmt, 3, SQL_C_SLONG, &versionMinor, 0, 0 );
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, STMT_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
}
}
if( !result )
{
retcode = SQLFetch( m_hstmt );
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, STMT_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
else
{
str_to_uc_cpy( pimpl.m_serverVersion, version );
pimpl.m_versionMajor = (int) versionMajor;
pimpl.m_versionMinor = (int) versionMinor;
}
}
}
}
}
if( result == 1 )
{
retcode = SQLEndTran( SQL_HANDLE_DBC, m_hdbc, SQL_ROLLBACK );
}
else
{
retcode = SQLEndTran( SQL_HANDLE_DBC, m_hdbc, SQL_COMMIT );
}
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, CONN_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
retcode = SQLFreeHandle( SQL_HANDLE_STMT, m_hstmt );
if( retcode != SQL_SUCCESS && retcode != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, STMT_ERROR );
result = 1;
}
m_hstmt = 0;
}

[/code]

results in:

 

[code]

entry.title: Use More than 6 characteristic data in ePPDS and generate setup matrix and run the optimizer
entry.content:
Hello ePPDS Experts,   

I am new in ePPDS module so i need your valuable input .  standard SAP is allow only 6 characteristics and generate the setup matrix based on that.   My requirement is to use more than 6 characteristics(total 9 char) and  generate the setup matrix with more than 3000 combination and feed it into optimizer. How can i achive it? please need your expert suggestion on this may be in little bit detail level as i am new in eppds area..  

I checked everywhere SAP is suggesting to use 6 char only and use combine(Concatenate) other char into it. and use it. But that one is not accepting. today they may have 9 char for this country, tomorrow for other country they may have more than that. may be 12 char. So need one proper solution where more than 9 char also can be accomodate  in roll out scenario for other countries. 

Need your expert advice on this. 

SCM APO Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling (PP/DS) 

 

Thanks,

Jowar Saha

entry.title: How to Create a Provider Contract in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Similar to SAP Subscription Billing
entry.content:
We have SAP Subscription Billing integrated with SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Sales Billing.

For a specific business case, we need to create a Provider Contract explicitly from a custom application / integration layer.

We checked communication scenario SAP_COM_0167, but it does not meet our requirement because it creates a normal Contract Accounting Provider Contract, not a Sales Billing Provider Contract. Also, SAP_COM_0167 does not provide fields to pass Solution Order and Solution Order Item references.

Our requirement is to create a Provider Contract in the same way SAP Subscription Billing creates it in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, so that the contract is available in the Sales Billing Provider Contract application/process.

We would like to understand:

  1. Is there any SAP-supported API, communication scenario, or business event to create a Sales Billing Provider Contract explicitly?

  2. Can a custom application / CPI publish the required inbound event/message through SAP Event Mesh, similar to the SAP Subscription Billing integration?

  3. Is the inbound event topic and payload schema publicly available for this provider contract creation process?

  4. If direct creation is not supported, what is the recommended SAP-supported approach for this scenario?

Any guidance, documentation, or implementation experience would be highly appreciated.

entry.title: Stop Searching, Start Asking: 3 Ways Joule is Changing the SAP Game!
entry.content:
SAP’s integration of Joule, the generative AI assistant, across Business Transformation Management solutions is quietly reshaping productivity for enterprise teams. Here are 3 actionable insights every SAP user and decision-maker should know:

1️⃣ Be Conversational - Stop hunting for information. Just ask Joule.

Whether you’re working in SAP LeanIX or SAP Signavio, Joule lets you locate process diagrams, fact sheets, reports, and accelerators using plain, everyday language. No complex navigation. No wasted time. Just conversational AI that instantly retrieves what you need.

2️⃣ Be Contextual - Automate routine tasks through chat-based workflows.

From creating and deleting journey models in SAP Signavio to managing to-dos and subscriptions in SAP LeanIX, Joule handles transactional tasks directly through the copilot interface. This isn’t just convenience. It’s a genuine productivity multiplier for your teams.

3️⃣Be Connected - Break down silos between SAP and non-SAP systems.

Powered by WalkMe, the SAP Joule Action Bar proactively engages users based on context, across any application, SAP or non-SAP. This means seamless, always-on AI guidance regardless of where your workflows live.

The future of enterprise productivity can be summed up in 3 Cs:

✔️Conversational — interact naturally, in plain language
✔️Contextual — get guidance tailored to exactly where you are
✔️Connected — work fluidly across every application in your landscape

🤔Which of the 3 Cs do you think would have the biggest immediate impact on your team’s productivity? And why?

entry.title: Re: No Successor for FOR FIORI APP id F3556 F3557
entry.content:
Hello,

Looks like the BC-CCM-DAG team already shared information about your concern.

Please read the note 2869647-Guidance for use of Data Aging in SAP S/4HANA.
As described in the note, for application data, we find that customers’ intended access to data is very often incompatible with the performance characteristics of aged data.
Section Further Technical Considerations in this note, talks about the other methodologies that can be used instead of data aging.

There will be no further developments in data aging. We are providing the support to our existing customer for data aging.

Best regards,
Joe

entry.title: Support Request: Enabling Capital Increase Functionality for Product Category 330 (Product Type 33E)
entry.content:
The Requirement

We need to enable the "Capital Increase" functionality for loans customized under Product Type 33E (which falls under Product Category 330).

Current Status and Technical Constraints

I have attempted several configuration steps in SPRO to facilitate this via transaction FN8X, but have encountered the following issues:

  1. UI Limitations: Despite the configuration attempts, the expected folder/tab for Capital Increase does not appear in the transaction as anticipated.
  2. Standard Restrictions: According to the SPRO documentation, the "Capital Increase" configuration appears to be natively supported only for Product Category 360.
  3. Flow Type Workaround: I attempted to define a new Flow Type that mimics the behavior of the original disbursement flow. However, this resulted in various inconsistency errors and "Invalid Entry" messages during execution.

I would appreciate your recommendation on how to correctly implement a Capital Increase for Product Category 330. Is there a specific component or business function that needs to be activated, or is my current configuration for Category 330 missing a critical dependency?

 

Business Process Description (To-Be State)

The objective is to increase the loan principal during an active lifecycle. The additional amount should be added to the current net principal balance on the date of the increase, with the remaining installments recalculated based on the original loan conditions.

 

Example Scenario:

  • Original Loan: 1,000 ILS.
  • Repayment Plan: 10 equal principal installments of 100 ILS each + fixed interest of 10 ILS per installment.
  • Trigger Event: After 2 installments have been paid (Remaining Balance = 800 ILS), an additional 500 ILS is granted to the customer.
  • Post-Increase Calculation:
    • New Principal Balance: 1,300 ILS (800 + 500).
    • Remaining Installments: 8.
    • New Principal Installment: 162.5 ILS (Original 100 + 62.5 from the increase).
    • New Interest Payment: 15 ILS (Original 10 + 5 from the increase).

entry.title: Issue in Standard Fiori App F1780 – “CO Area is Not Set” (Cost Accountant – Production)
entry.content:
Hi Folks,

I am writing this blog after analyzing the issue in detail and discussing it with the SAP Product Team. I hope this helps others resolve the issue quickly and efficiently.

 

Issue Description:

We activated the standard Fiori application F1780 – Cost Accountant – Production in our S/4HANA system. However, after configuring the app in the Fiori Launchpad (FLP), the following error appeared directly on the application tile:

"CO Area is not set"

 

Additionally, when clicking the tile and attempting to launch the application, the system displayed a warning message and did not allow further navigation.

 

Analyzing:

During troubleshooting, we identified that the issue was related to the Controlling Area assignment in the Fiori Launchpad user profile.

SAP Note referenced:

2403512 – Controlling area is not set for the user in Fiori Launchpad

 

Based on the note, we maintained the Controlling Area in the FLP user settings/profile. However, even after maintaining the configuration, the same error and warning continued to appear.

 

Solution:

After further discussion with the SAP Product Team, they provided the correct resolution and created the following KBA:

3753053 – F1780: “Controlling Area is not set” error in Fiori Launchpad

As per the KBA, the issue can be resolved by maintaining the required parameter in transaction SU01.

Steps to Fix

Navigate to:

SU01 → User Profile → Parameters

Maintain the following entry:

  • SET/GET Parameter ID: C4C

  • Parameter Value: 8400 (Use your relevant Controlling Area value)

 

After saving the changes:

  1. Log out from the Fiori Launchpad
  2. Log in again

The error message on the tile will disappear, and the application will open successfully with the expected data.

 

 

After Clicking on the TIle, it will show the data

 

Conclusion:

The issue is not resolved only by maintaining the Controlling Area in FLP personalization settings. The crucial step is maintaining the C4C parameter in SU01 for the respective user.

I hope this blog saves time for others facing the same issue.

Please feel free to share your suggestions or additional findings.

Regards,
Rohit Gera
